summary:
Dhra is a Pre-Pottery Neolithic A site in the Jordan Valley, located south of the Dead Sea in modern Jordan, dated to approximately 9500–9000 BCE. The site is notable for the discovery of some of the world's earliest known purpose-built granaries — substantial round structures with raised floors supported by wooden beams, designed for the bulk storage of harvested grain. These granaries at Dhra appear to slightly predate even the Jericho settlement in their development of formal storage facilities, and are among the earliest evidence anywhere for the systematic storage of agricultural surplus.

The granaries at Dhra were capable of holding several tonnes of grain, implying that the community was already producing consistent surpluses sufficient to warrant dedicated storage infrastructure. This early evidence of grain storage is significant because surplus storage is considered a key driver of social change: it enables populations to survive lean seasons, supports non-food-producing specialists, and creates conditions for the emergence of social inequality and hierarchy. Alongside [[event:1385|the earliest known granaries]] from other sites in the region, Dhra contributes to understanding the early economic organization of the world's first farming communities in the Jordan Valley.
